We have received from the Catholic Defence Society,
11 Lower Dominick Street, Dublin, an interesting re-
port headed ¢ Light on the Local Government Board.”’
Of the nominated salaried posts 34 are held by
““ Saved *’ and only 13 by ‘‘ Idolators ”’; the solicitors
to the Board are non-Catholics. The 34 ‘ Saved’’
draw, approximately, £22,225 and the 13 Idz_)lators 2
about £7,550—the averages work out respectively at
£653 and £680.  The ‘‘ Saved ”’ have it every way.

“auditorships are nominated jobs, and amongst thzsi;
é[‘elt)::i:n of the sl'e?l'vice out of 17 onlyf five are ‘‘Idolators
__all probably of the ‘ tame ’ variety. As regards the
staff that got into the service by competitive examina-
tions—leaving out minor appointments helow S’?cond
Division rank—we find there‘are 43 I_dolators ;_md
95 ‘Saved.” Taken in conjunction with the figures

concerning the nominated jobs, the state of affairs in
connection with the competitive examination portion of
the staff tells its own plain tale. But it is one thing
for an ““ Idolator”’ to gain entrance to the Local Gov.
ernment Board as to the Great Northern and Creat
Sourface Railways, it is another matter when the
authorities deal with promotions. The 43 ““ Idolators’’
receive about £8,930 ; the 25 ‘“ Saved ’’ about £7,610—
averages, ‘‘ Idolators,” £207 13s. 6d.; ‘“ Saved,’”’ £304
8s. 0d. Evidently, though Papists get in by examina-
tion, the well-known law of “the survival of the ‘Saved’ ’’
operates subsequently.  Though the total figures are
43 to 25, when we come to examine the higher
grade of the Second Division and the staff positions the
proportion is softened by the operation of the law of
““ the survival of the ‘ Saved’”’ to 15 ‘“ Idolators’’ to
14 ““ Saved,”” and the average salary of the 15 is about
£390, whilst that of the 14 “ Saved’’ is about £457.
In the nominated jobs the proportion of the ‘“ Idola-
tors * to the “ Saved "’ is 13 to 35 ; in the competitive—
leaving out anything lower than the Second Division—
the proportion is 43 to 25—a remarkable revolution;
but amongst the latter, when the operation of the law
of the survival of the ‘“ Saved’’ has had time to operate,
we find that in the higher posts of the competitive staff
the proportion softens from 15 to 14, the ayerage of the
15 being-about £67 below that of the 14. The Catholic
Defence Society is to be congratulated on this report
which it has compiled, and we trust that ““ Light on
the Local Government Board’’ is, as it were, only
No. 1, Vol. I, of a series of similar and badly needed
reports of the working of the law of the ‘“survival of
the ‘Saved.””’

We felt like rubbing our eyes when we saw all the
fuss made about Mr. Sympathy Bryce in the Simply
Deplorable of Saturday last. There is something
about it all that recalls the ““ Union of Hearts’’ days.
The leading article in the Deplorable commences this
way: —‘ Mr. Bryce’s appearance at the National Lite-
rary Society as lecturer on an interesting aspect of old
Irish history was a graceful and characteristic conclu-
sion to his Chief Secretaryship. It will undoubtedly
increase the regret that he should have been called
away to a sphere of duty which is deemed to have more
urgent claims upon him.”” There is not much trace of
independent opposition about that! Why should Ire-
land regret that Mr. Sympathy Bryce was called away ?
What has he done whilst he was here beyond giving us
sympathetic replies? -The article in .the Deplorable is
quite a eulogy of Mr. Bryce. What is the meaning of
all this? g

Reading between the lines it would appear that the
Deplorable suggests that the game on is, that Mr.
Bryce is to smooth the way for an Anglo-Yankee under-
standing. Now, if there is any, ‘“ deal ”’ of that sort in
the making, the people ought to be taken into con-
fidence. The Deplorable and Mr. John Dillon, flopping
down before Mr. Sympathy Bryce, suggest at least the
question—what is the price—what are they getting for
it? If they tell us what they are getting—if they are
getting anything—the public can form an opinion as to
whether they are performing a silly flop or are making
a business ‘“deal.”” If the Irish nation could, as it
were, sell an Anglo-Yankee understanding for value
received by the Irish nation, it would be a proposal for
serious consideration. But the Irish people ought to
know the particulars, in so far as there may be parti-
culars, of the “deal.”” The Deplorable winds up its
article in this way:—‘¢ While regretting his departure,

therefore, let us hope that hesmay yet find the chance

We all recollect the awe with which we viewed our 
schoolmasters when we were boys; for, however kindly

feeling of awe. 
One (?f the most delightful functions 
of budding manhood is perhaps when, having left your

We are sorry to hear of the death of Mr, Buckley, a
well-known County Cork teacher, and we tender our
sympathy to his family. It is only two years since Mr.
Buckley retired on pension after half a century of
strenuous work. At the time of his retirement we took
occasion to refer to his very exceptional services to edu-
cation and the Irish language. A capable Irish scholar
he taught Irish throughout the greater part of his life
in and out of school, and constantly advocated it with
speech and pen. He was a man of wide knowledge, and
a very successful teacher. In addition to his work as
principal of a large school he conducted special classes
in Science, and for the Civil Service and other profes-
sions, and his old pupils, many of whom are now suc-
cessful men in various professions at home and abroad,
will hear of his death with regret. His colleagues and
his countrymen should be proud of his record. It is
men of his stamp who do most to inform and maintain
the true Irish spirit, and in recent times it is mainly
to such men we owe it that the Irish Ireland movement
has made such headway. He left many friends, but no
enemies. There were very general signs of mourning
and respect in the district. A touching tribute was
that paid by a large number of his old pupils who con-
veyed the coffin on their shoulders the whole five miles
journey to the cemetery over snow and ice-covered roads,
the hearse going idly ahead.

A reader went to the Ribbon, Flower and Feather
Department of one of the monster drapery houses of
Dublin, and bought some flowers. When the purchaser
went home it was found that the paper bag in which the
flowers were enclosed had the imprint of a London firm.
‘We note, however, that the bag, which is before us, is
patented, the invention in the case being, as far as we
can dce, the insertion of a piece of green twine—the
twine you observe is green—through the top of the bag,
so that by pulling it the top of the bag is closed. If
the firm wants this class of bag, of course, the bag being
protected by patent, it must get it from the firm that

When the Intermediate results were announced, we
referred to the brilliant success of St. Louis Convent
Monaghan. We are glad to note that at the recent dis-
tribution of prizes in connection with the convent, Dr.
Hyde’s play, “ The Bursting of the Bubble,”” was per-
formed, the actors being pupils of the school.

We have just received a copy of the revised prospectus
of the St. Kevin’s Holiday House and Domestic Economy
Training School for Catholic Girls, which is situate at
St. Kevin’s Park, Kilmacud, Stillorgan, Co. Dublin.
We take the following from the prospectus:—“ This is
the only training school of the kind in Ireland where
students have the great advantage of a ‘living’ house,
conducted by lay persons experienced in household
management, with a constant succession of guests giv-
ing the classeS full practice in cookery, laundry work,
and housewifery. Demonstration and practice are com-
bined in these important branches; and in addition to
cutting-out and dressmaking, lectures are given. on
household management, first aid, and sick mursing.
The students are taught by two first-class instructresses,
Miss S. Perry and Miss C. Lynch, who hold the highest
diplomas in domestic science under the Department of
Agriculture and Technical Instruction.”

ETHE RAILWAY COMMISSION.

HE meetings of the Railway Commission have
been resumed. The directors are now repre-
sented by Counsel. Rumour has it that Mr. Balfour
Brown, K.C., received a thousand pounds (£1,000) with
his brief, and that his daily “ refresher ”” is £100. As
the Irish Bar has been supposed to have a reputation
equal to the Bar of any country, it seems strange that
the directors could not find an Irish Barrister to do
the work now done by Mr. Brown. To a looker-on,
the work, so far, seems very little, and such as could
be done by any intelligent Barrister.

It is strange that the Public Bodies in Ireland have
not employed Counsel. Next to the land, the rail-
ways are, or could be made, the most important factor
in the production of wealth. They may soon be under
the control of a National representative body; and the
existing representative bodies should feel interested in
the evidence on which the report of the Commissioners
will be based. Hitherto the evidence has bezen of a
very varied character. Mr. O’Dea (Dublin)—and no
evidence yet given has been more interesting than his
—stated that the railway rates shut out his home manu-
factured article (bedsteads, etc.) from four counties.
Mr. Power (Dungarvan) showed that the original high
rates for home-grown apples would, if maintained, have
crushed out an infant industry (cider), and that a re-
duction in rates was very slowly granted. Mr. Wise
(Cappoquin) dwelt on the injury done to that town
and locality by the existing traffic arrangements. From
Cork, Limerick, Enniskillen, Sligo, etc., there have also
been interesting witnesses; but up to the present most
of the evidence has dealt, perhaps necessarily, with de-
tails affecting these particular localities. The Railway
Managers will, by and, bye, contradict or explain the
statements made, and it is in the examination of such
officials, and in the final summing up of the evidence,
that the absence of friendly Counsel will be felt.

The personality of each of the members of the Com-
mission is gradually impressing itself on the audience.
The Chairman, Sir Charles Scotter, is thoroughly con-
versant with all railway matters. At first he was a
good deal puzzled by his want of acquaintance with
Trish geography; Clones and Thurles were as unfami-
liar to him as places in ‘Western China; and Collooney.
and Fermoy required much searching for on the map;
but he is steadily adding to his information in this
respect. The Chairman is a good examiner; he takes
a witness ably ‘over the chief points in the type-

that he has his own views and opinions, he does nof
obtrude them. 43
Lord Pirrie has a good-natured, beaming expression
of countenance, often lighted up by a smile which is as
open and joyous as that of a boy in his teens. As an

3 5 &

examiner he aims solely at getting informaticn. He
has no wish whatever to puzzle a witness. On the con-
trary, he tries to assist those whom he questions by put-
ting them at their ease. It is plain that he has an im-
mense grasp of commercial transactions on a large scale,
but he is not equally familiar with the small de'ails of
Irish commercial life.

Mr. Ackworth is “a lawyer all over.””  He often com-
mences his examination of a witness by secking for a
definition of some term previously used, and he employs
this definition to test some principle advanced by the
witness. The witness soon finds himself “in a corner.”
Some witnesses half-sulkily remain in the corner, others
try to scramble out of it as best they can.

From the newspapers it would be thought that the
audience consists solely of railway directors and mana-
gers, but others also attend regularly, and visitors are
constantly coming and going. Should a person enter
who is familiar with the proceedings; his first question,
in a subdued whisper, is—‘ Who is the witness up?’
If a‘stranger comes, he settles down, and then after a
short time whispers %o his neighbour—*“ Which is Mr.
Sexton?” The neighbour indicates Mr. Sexton by say-
ing that he is sitting next the Chairman, and the visitor,
in most cases, fixes on Lord Pirrie, who sits on the
Chairman’s left, Mr. Sexton being on the Chairman’s
right. This causes some confusion which a few addi-
tional whispers set right, and the visitor rcmains auiet
for some time, having ““a good look ’’ at Mr. Sexton.

The other members of the Commission do not take as
prominent a part as those named, but all give close
attention to the evidence. Colonel Hutchinson-Poe
has, unfortunately, been absent for some time. = When
in attendance he questioned with much fulness on cer-
tain points. Mr. Aspinall questions definitely and pre-
cisely. Sir Herbert Ilkyll was not present at the earlier
meetings, and so far has been chiefly a listener.

Thatt the Report of the Commission may be of im-
mense importance to the country seems extremely prob-
able. Already it is plain that the smaller railways, as
separate entities, must go. Nor does there seem to be
room to doubt that the Commission will report in favour
of one—amalgamated—system of Irish railways. The
most important question seems to be—Is the Irish
system of railways to be managed by an Imperial De-
partment, such as the Board of Works, or by an Irish
representative body employing suitable experts?’ For
the due consideration of this immensely important ques-
tion the employment of counsel to act for the existing
Irish representative bodies seems essential.

THE CATHOLIC TRUTH SOCIETY.

SRMIT me, while paying my meed of praise to the
excellent and artistic manner in which the
Catholic Truth Annual has been produced, to, at the
same time, enter a respectful but strong protest against
the inclusion therein of the photographs of the artistes
who merely took part in the musical function in connec-
tion with the Reception at the Gresham.

In making this protest I must not be understood as
attempting to detract in the smallest degree from the
merits, or the reputation, or the status of any of those
ladies and gentlemen. But thereisa time and a place for
everything, and except alone, if even then, that these
artistes were subscribing members of the Catholic Truth
Society, and, in addition, gave their services gratis, the
place for their photographs much less the free advertise-
ment, in theatrical style, of their very photographers;
was not, I would submit, the pages of the Catholic
Truth Annual.

It was bad enough—though an apology is now made
for it—that somebody should have blundered in con-
nection with the arrangements for the promenade con-

_cert.  Parenthetically, could it be that consideration

for the susceptibilities of the artistes in regard to inter-
ruption—as well as for the comfort of some members of
the audience—was a determining factor in this fauz
pas, Be that, however, as it may, the climax is

d by the sandwiching in of these photographs

JANUARY 12, 1907

in the Annual. It reminds one of the time, before the
introduction® of the Gregorian Chant, when, in great
religicus ceremonies, the sanctuary played only second
fiddle to the organ loft, and poor pressmen had to be
more careful in dealing with the musical side of the
function than in reporting the sermon.

I cortainly do not think that members of the Catholic
Truth Society, who have borne the h_eat; and toil of the
day, will in future deem the insertion of their photo-
graphs in the Annual other than a very dubious
honour, indeed.

And surely it is but a poor compliment to such a
distinguished man as Dr. MacCaffrey, Professor of
Ecclesiastical History at Maynooth, who read such a
scholarly paper at the Conference, that he should be
given quite a back seat in comparison with those
musicians and vocalists.

If illustrative padding was deemed desirable in the
Annual, why not have given us the courteous and
deserving stewards—who, besides being members of the
Catholic Truth Society, are also zealous workers in
other Catholic bodies in Dublin—or the authors, many
of them distinguished, who have contributed interest-
ing booklets.

In expressing my opinion on this matter, I do not
wish to be understood as implying censure on any of
the. hard-working officials of the Society, honorary or
otherwise. The responsibility, such as it is, must rest
on our large and representative Committee of Manage-
ment, from whom a more discriminating appreciation
of the fitness of things might be expected.

Another matter of which I beg to complain is this:
At the 1905 annual meeting a lay member of the Coun-
cil suggested that booklets dealing with interesting
epochs, or distinguished characters in Irish history,
should be published by the Society. The Archbishop
of Tuam, President of the Society, who was in the chair
at the time, not only expressed his sympathy with the
suggestion, but he has, once, at least, since, adverted to
it in terms of approval. And all the leading Catholic
and National organs in the Irish press wrote warmly
in favour of the proposal when it was originally made.
It was understood, also, that prizes would be given for
suitable historical booklets.

Well, after an incubation of a year and a half, the
Scheme of - prizes—five in number—now appears on
page 15 of the current Annual. Four of these come
under the head of historical subjects, though, strictly
speaking—if T exclude ‘‘ Catholic Emancipation ’’—
not more than two—the first and second—fall within
this sphere. And what think you are the important
epochs or distinguished characters placed in the very
forefront? They are:—I. ‘“ The Invasion of Ireland
by Edward Bruce ”’; and, II. ‘ The Life and Achieve-
ments of the Red Earl of Ulster.”” Now, may I ask,
could two more uninteresting subjects have been found,
or could the National Board, itself, have done worse in
making a selection? The first has little, if any, in-
terest for Irishmen in general, and for intrinsic reasons
there is actually something malapropos from the re-
ligious point of view in resurrecting that particular
event in our history. Then, how many of your intel-
ligent readers can tell me right off who the “ Red Barl
of Ulster ”’ was? For my own part, though I thought
I knew something of Irish history, it took me a whole
day ransacking my memory before I finally fixed on
him. I had the assistance of a friend, a first prize-
taker three years in succession in Gaelic League his-
torical competitions. We tried in turn to make the
title fit Hugh O’Neill, Earl of Tyrone; Owen Roe
O’Neill, and Red Hugh O’Donnell, but failed. Then
we fell back on John De Courcy, but with no better
result. My prize-winner friend gave up the quest as
hopeless. At long last, from the rearmost recesses of
a rather projecting pole, where the unimportant odds
and ends of my life memories séem to hide themselves
away, I drew forth a vague recollection of a Red De
Burgo. I went at once to the copious index in Father
Dalton’s Trish history, and the mystery was solved.'
Now, not to labour the matter unduly: This is not
the class of Irish history that is wanting in the coun

wigorously, others who advocated letting it rest. Cer-

 JANUARY 12, 1907.

It will not be bought;, and I am speaking from long

experience as a Catholic Truth Society worker; so it
is sheer waste of money to print it. Not even if ‘ The
Invasion of Bruce’’ and ‘‘ The Life of the Red Earl ”
were embodied in one booklet (as they well might be,
seeing they were contemporary acts in the same drama)
would it be likely to sell.

Thg way to induce our people to cultivate a taste for
the history of their own country is—unless the reverse
is deliberately contemplated—first, to give them the
Lives of, say, Brian Boru, Hugh O’Neill, Red Hugh
O’Donnell, Owen Roe, Patrick Sarsfield, and men of
that stamp, with the national events of their time
woven round their careers. Then, later on, the lacuna
could be filled in with less important characters, such
as this rieving, ruthless, Earla Ruadh.

Let our Committee of Management, living, though
most of them may be, their daily lives in (as a distin-
guished ecclesiastical confrére of theirs once described it)
““ the atmosphere through which the castle smoke dis-
sipates itself ’—let them, I would say, remember that
it is the legitimate wants and wishes of the faithful
Catholic masses they have to consider, and not the
susceptibilities of the Whigs or Tories or Devolutionists
on the Council. I always advise my clerical friends to
mind the masses—whatever the classes may do—and
the Church will never be left in the lurch in Ireland
as it has been in France.

_And laymen who are doing their best for Catholic
literature in the country, aud the preservation of
Catholic and clerical influences in the educational
sphere, are sadly hampered iu their work by national
obscurantism of the kind of which I complain, and of
which, T am sure, the overwhelming bulk of the clergy
and episcopacy do not approve; and against which I

would, in conclusion, reierate my respectful but
emphatic protest.

EXIT MR. BRYCE.

IRISH Chief Secretaries might fitly have as their
official epitaph: “I came, I saw, I went.” No
doubt one or two feel themselves entitled to have in-
scribed unaltered on the tombstone of their adminis-
trative record the proud words of the first Cemsar;
Walter Long and Arthur Balfour feel, perhaps, and
their admirers surely think, that they did triumph,
and that in a further paraphrase (this time of the
second Cwmsar) they found Ireland of leagues and they
left it of policemen. But even in the case of those ad-
ministrators of the Law, who, by a display of
““strength,’’ excite the admiration of Orange sup-
porters, the most striking feature of their career is the
rapidity with which it terminated. Whether the Chief
BSecretary believes in twenty years of resolute Govern-
ment. or in Home Rule, whether he favours a scheme
of killing Home Rule by kindness, or holds ideas of
devolution with a view of leading up to a larger policy,
whether-he be an apostle of a new era, or a devotee to
the maintenance of “Law and Order,”” a year or two
at most is all that is allotted to him in which to give
-expression to his views. 5

In about twenty years Ireland has had the benefit of

‘being ruled by eight Chief Secretaries, and we are
now about to be honoured with a ninth. It should be
remembered that during this period there have prac-
tically been only three Governments—that is to say,
the Tory Party has held power for seventeen out of
the twenty years, and the Liberals for little more than
three. Perhaps we should feel grateful to the pre-
dominant partner for so generously supplying us with
Statesmen to look after our affairs—in a multitude of
Counsellors there is wisdom. Yet an adage touching
cooks and broth may also come to mind as being per-

not inapplicable. Surely if too many professors

‘of the culinary art may do damage, the ~br9§h of Zl;nsh

administration is in parlous danger of being §poxle§.

We have had cooks who believed in stirring it

THE  LEADER. 339

tain chefs appeared to hold that it should be flavoured
strongly with Orange—rather a remarkable recipe—
others had an idea of‘adding ingredients likely to make
it more palatable to ‘the popular taste. Then we have
had some who thought it should be hot, others who
believe it tastes better cold. Again, the kitchen
authorities appear on some occasions to have deemed
that in a Catholic country something in the nature of-a
fasting soup for consumption on Fridays and fast days
should be provided, while others have insisted that no
such pandering to Sectarian views should be allowed,
and that the one ‘concoction should suffice for all.

The result has been what one would naturally anti-
cipate. No one has heen pleased. A momentary joy
has possibly been experienced by some when the fare
offered proved palatable for a year or two; then, alas,
came a change, and joy was turned to sorrow while the
digestions of all suffered as a consequence of the vary-
ing diet. Moreover, it is not only those who have to
partake of the broth that are dissatisfied, but the other
persons employed in its manufacture, the permanent
scullions, as it were, find it difficult to adapt them-
selves to so many different cooks, and often prefer to
follow their own sweet wills vather than to carry out
the diverse instructions given to them. Hence further
obstacles in the way of satisfactory catering.

That the proper way for remedying this state of
affairs is to reform it altogether, and do away with it;
all shades of Nationalist opinion are agreed. Pending,
however, a settlement of our demands by a National
Parliament and Executive, it is at least to be wished
that more stability might be imparted to the office of
Chief Secretary. Some months ago in 7'he New Ireland
Review there appeared an article, by Arthur Synan,
suggesting a scheme whereby this official might be
elected by the Irish people. The scheme was novel and
ingenious, but not exactly within the somewhat restricted
sphere of practical politics. It may, moreover, be
objected that when there is a particular goal to be aimed
for, the less deviation there is from the straight path
leading in that direction the better. This, however, by
the way only. I have not Mr. Synan’s article by me,
and am not therefore in a position to discuss its various
points. I have only alluded to it as it bears upon the
subject.

A% the present moment the Irish office is'a strange
position. Mr. Bryce, whose name has become almost as
much a synonym for sympathy in this country as that of
Mr, Arthur Balfour was at one time for a far shorter
word of less pleasant significance, was generally credited
with good intentions.  These have in this instance
paved the path to Washington instead of the floor of a
more remote and warmer region. It remains to be seen
.whether, as he himself expressed a hope on last Friday
at the Antient Concert Rooms, his successor will carry
these into effect, but his departure af this juncture is
not a very hopeful augury for the future. It was very
generally rumoured that he was engaged during the
pash year in the preparation for the coming session of
some kind of Bill dealing with Irish government, and
his removal from the scene a month before the meeting:
of Parliament is calculated to induce qualms in the
breasts of those who exttended a show of clemency to the
Government on this understanding.  For the present
let us give Mr. Bryce fthe credit of having meant well,
since at-least we have nothing to charge against him. A
Liberal Chief Secretary has a far harder card to play
than a Unionist—a fact which should be taken into con-
sideration by those who murmur at the shortcomings of
tho former. Let us hope that he will find no cause for
disappointment in the actions of his successor as, like
tthe hurler on the fence, he watches them from the hub
of the American Commonwealth. Avis.

THINK it well to preface this article with two pro-
nouncements quoted in former ones, so as to keep
before the eyes of the public the issues that are at
stake regarding the primary education of the country.
_ The first is that of the Catholic Bishops at their meet-
ing at Maynooth on the 22nd of June, 1904 :—

Any limitation or restriction of the control which is now exer-
cised by managers over the schools of the National system of
education would be so injurious to the religious interests of educa-
tion of the people, as to make imperative on us to resist such a
measure, and, in case it wero adopted, to reconsider our whole

. position in relation to those schools.

The second is that of the Central Council of Clerical

~ Managers : —

That any change in the constitution or reconstruction of tho
‘National Bo.nr‘d, or any change in its rules, will be uncompro-
n:mm‘gly yasisted by us if it, in the elightest degreo, impair the
right of managers in the appointnient and dismissal of toachers as
at present arranged by tho Bishops of Ireland, and in the super-
vision and safeguarding of the faith and morals of tha pupils

~ during their attendance at the school.

“Jacqties’ ” “ uniform cohetent scheme,’’ so far as it
ffects primary education with which I am dealing, is
y opposed to these two pronouncements from the

For, in the first place, as was pointed out in my last,
he proposes to deprive the managers at once of the right
of appointment and dismissal of the teachers; and
sccondly, he boldly advocates the starting of local rates
to supplement the Government grants for educational
purposes, which, in all countries, leads to the placing of
local bodies in control of the schools. He lightly brushes
aside, ab pages 62 and 63, the findings of the Financial
Relations Commission, according to which we are over-
taxed to the tune of a few millions every year; he
ignores, too, the annual pilfering of this nation by the
British Treasury, of its fair share of the education
grants voted by Parliament, and he seriously advises us
to lie down tamely under this injustice, and begin ab
once to- tax ourselves, and let the predominant partner
continue her policy of plunder.

This coming from one who poses as a friend and coun-
sellor of the Irish nation is bad enough, but in its bear-
ing on the position of the clerical managers, it would
mean in the long run their complete elimination, which,
try to conceal it as he may, is the ultimate aim of
“Jacques ’’ and his pamphlet.

To some of my readers it will doubtless seem strange
that I should devote so much time and labour to the
review of a mere pamphlet. My reason for doing go is
this: —I am convinced that this pamphlet was written
for a purpose, and that purpose is—a revolution in the
primary education of the country, of which we hear the
distant rumblings from time to time on public plat-
forms. “Jacques’’ undertook a difficult task; he wished
to gather all kinds of fish into his met—the Govern-
ment, the teachers, the managers, the public bodies,
Protestants and Catholics, all alike. He wanted to
stand on many stools at the same time. He overrated
his capacity, and fell into many contradictions and in-
consistencies.  But whilst this is true, it cannot be
denied or ignored that the fundamental principles under-
lying his scheme are now being proclaimed and pushed
forward by certain agencies through the country. And
it is for this reason that I have undertaken to analyse
to some extent “ Jacques’ >’ “ uniform coherent’’ scheme
of education for Treland, so as to be able to let in some

light on the operation of these agencies.  There aro
three such at work to my mind.

And first in the order of time, as well,
perhaps, as of importance, is a weekly school

journal started last year soon after the appear-
ance of the “Jacques’’ pamphlet. In its early issues
it was harmless enough. As it advanced in life some
things appeared to which exception may be taken by
some, but in its issue of August 18th an article appeared
which startled its readers by its open and undisguised
advocacy of local rating, such as was proposed 1n the
“ Jacques scheme.”  The following are the exact words
of the article in question :—

until they are made to pay for it, they will ncver appreciate it.

.—and much would be accomplished by persistent offort on the part.
of the teachers, aided, as we are certain they would be, by tho
different branches of the Gaelic Leaguc—a reform made in depart-
ment administration, and an additional grant voted by the Govern-
ment—and this has been actually promised—the position of the

THE LIBRARIES QUESTION.

A S my speech at the lecture on Freo Libraries at the

Calaroga Club has been a subject of unfavour-
. able comment by some of your contributors, it is only
to be expected that I should try to set myself right
before them and the public generally. I may then say,
L first of all, that no report of it was given by the Press—
there was merely a culogistic comment on an incidental
remark made during the course of the speech to the
effect that anything, including the Free Library, that
\\.muld lessen the evil influence of the private circulating
library should be welcomed. To show that my speech
was far more against the principle of the Free Library
f?tl’lan for it, I herewith give the following account of
15—

Tn discussing the introduction of the Free Library
into provincial towns and country districts, we should
be guided by practical as well as theoretic considera-
tions. My experience in some of the towns in which
the Free Library had been working for years was un-
favourable to it—men crowded into a small reading-
room for the betting news, a certain number of young
women coming in principally on Saturday evenings for
novels, good books of which a fair share existed entirely
neglected. My experience in America and England did
not convince me of their literary supremacy over our
own country, ceteris paribus, in spite of Mr. Lyster’s
appreciation of them as “book-provided ”’ countries in
contrast with Ireland. As regards America I had seen
largo libraries, but failed to discover a corresponding
large number of readers, and deplored the absence of the
real literary spirit in the United States and Canada.
As regards what I said in favour of the I'ree Library, I
held that what was bad in one town might be good in
another, under a different set of circumstances, and I
acknowledged that the Drogheda Free Library, where T
was present at the opening by his Eminence, the Car-
dinal, appealed to me as the ideal of what a Free Library
should be. The selection of books, made almost entirely
by an intelligent Catholic layman, could not be sur-
passed, fiction occupying a relatively small position.
The reading-room, provided with good newspapers and
magazines, amongst which were the “Tablet’’ and
“ Donohoe’s Magazine,”” was frequented largely by
working men, and the strict observance of the rules
against speaking, smoking and spitting was an educat-
ing influence in itself. I then argued that as private
circulating, or subscription, libraries existed in every
town over which no supervision could be exercised, and
where, generally speaking, novels of a questionable

nature could be obtained by the subscribers, often with-
out the knowledge even of other'subscribers, and as, more-
over, the working classes were very much given to the
reading of papers like the Budget and Reynold’s N ews-
paper, an ideal library, such as that opened by his
Lminence under Catholic management should be wel-
comed by all who had the spiritual and intellectual
interests of the people at heart.

This rough outline of my speech will go to show how
I can reconcile the apparent anomaly of Cardinal Logue
opening a Free Library in one place and Monsignor
Hallinan opposing it in another. The circumstances of
the two towns were entirely different. No parochial
library existed in Drogheda, nor was it possible to estab-
lish one on an adequate scale. The newsagents provide
what is asked for without inquiry; private subscription
libraries exist, though, by the way, I declare that I do
not say anything directly against those in Drogheda
except that they belong to what, is in general, an un-
desirable class. Was it possible under these circum-
stances to refuse Mr. Carnegie’s gift? On the other
hand, in Newcastle West a good parochial library was
already in existence, and a Free Library was not only
unnecessary, but would have introduced discord and
opposition. From the financial point of view, it would
seem the right thing, in a small place especially, to im-
prove a library already in existence than to establish a
new one. If other priests were to follow the Mon-
signor’s example, and establish a good Catholic library
on a generous scale, with reading-room attached, it
would save much heart-burning in the near future, and
solve the reading problem as regards small country
towns and villages. TaE CHAIRMAN.
